196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}

480	\section{Lean encoding}
481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}
523	
524	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
525	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
526	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
527	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
528	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).
529

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
191	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp at the pinned Mathlib commit
192	% b80f227 carries a constant-functor placeholder body
193	% ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const _).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of PUnit)),
194	% i.e.\ the constant functor at the trivial abelian group. This is NOT
195	% the relative Picard presheaf
196	% \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) that the definition
197	% above describes; the placeholder merely supplies a type-checking
198	% object of the correct kind. The closure is gated on the upstream
199	% Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap (no
200	% tensor-product \texttt{AddCommGroup} structure on
201	% \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} is available, hence no quotient group
202	% can be built on the Lean side until that gap is closed). DO NOT
203	% promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic \texttt{sync\_leanok}
204	% phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in review, until the body is
205	% replaced with the mathematically correct construction.
206	
207	\begin{lemma}\leanok
208	[Naturality respects the group structure]
209	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
213	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
214	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
215	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
216	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
217	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
220	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
221	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
222	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
223	  %  [\dots]
224	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
225	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
226	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
231	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
232	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
233	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
234	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
235	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
236	  homomorphisms.
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
241	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
242	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
243	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
244	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial at b80f227 carries a
245	% zero AddMonoidHom body (\(0 : \dots \to \dots\)), i.e.\ the zero map
246	% between two trivial-group endpoints, NOT the pullback-descended
247	% homomorphism this lemma describes. Moreover, because the codomain's
248	% \texttt{Zero} instance is derived from the sorry-body
249	% \texttt{addCommGroup} (the file-local genuine sorry of the chapter),
250	% the closure also inherits \texttt{sorryAx} via that typeclass leak;
251	% it is therefore not even axiom-clean. The closure is gated on the
252	% upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO
253	% NOT promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic
254	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in
255	% review, until the body is replaced with the mathematically correct
256	% map descended from \(g_C^*\).
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
265	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
266	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
267	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
268	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
269	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
270	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
271	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
272	\end{proof}
273	
274	\begin{theorem}\leanok
275	[\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]
276	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
278	  
279	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
280	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
281	  % abelian groups)
282	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
283	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
284	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
285	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
286	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
287	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
288	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
289	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
290	  The assignment \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
291	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of
292	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
293	  \[
294	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
295	                                     \AddCommGroup
296	  \]
297	  on the category of \(k\)-schemes.
298	\end{theorem}
299	
300	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with the prior theorems, Kleiman \S 2 does not supply
301	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
302	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
303	% above; no external proof is being translated.
304	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
305	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf at b80f227 is defined as
306	% \texttt{PicSharp \_C}, i.e.\ it re-exports the constant-PUnit-functor
307	% placeholder from \texttt{PicSharp} above; it is NOT the canonical
308	% presheaf assembled from the pullback-descended functorial action
309	% \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) that this theorem describes. The closure is
310	% gated on the upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules}
311	% monoidal-structure gap. DO NOT promote this block to \leanok via the
312	% deterministic \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it
313	% formalised in review, until the body is replaced with the
314	% mathematically correct presheaf assembly.
315	\begin{proof}
316	
317	  The object map is well defined by
318	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
319	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
320	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by
321	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
322	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor
323	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\), i.e.\ a presheaf of abelian groups on
324	  \((\Sch/k)\).
325	\end{proof}

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
339	
340	\begin{definition}\leanok
341	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
342	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
344	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
345	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
346	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
347	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
348	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
349	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
350	  % below is also corrected.
351	  
352	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
353	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
354	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
355	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
356	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
359	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
360	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
361	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
362	  % topologies  by
363	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
364	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
365	  %  \end{dfn}
366	  %  [\dots]
367	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
368	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
369	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
370	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
371	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
372	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
373	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
374	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
375	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
376	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
377	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
378	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
379	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
380	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
381	  \[
382	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
383	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
384	  \]
385	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
386	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
387	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
388	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
389	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
390	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
391	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
392	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
393	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
394	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
395	\end{definition}

499	
500	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
501	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
502	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
503	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the
504	  hom-set of presheaf morphisms between these two objects is therefore
505	  nonempty. The full universal property of this canonical morphism

512	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
513	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
514	
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
516	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
517	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
518	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
519	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
520	  % property)
521	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
523	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
524	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
525	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
526	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
527	  % topologies  by
528	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
529	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
530	  %  \end{dfn}
531	  %  [\dots]
532	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
533	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
534	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
535	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
536	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
537	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
538	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
539	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
540	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
541	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
542	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
543	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
544	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
545	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
546	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
547	  \[
548	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
549	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
550	  \]
551	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
552	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
553	  of presheaves of abelian groups
554	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
555	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
556	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
557	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
558	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
559	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
560	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
561	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
562	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
563	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
564	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
565	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
566	  \(\eta\).
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
570	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
571	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
572	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
573	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
574	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
575	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
576	\begin{proof}
577	
578	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
579	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
580	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
581	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
582	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
583	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
584	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
585	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
586	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
587	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
588	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
589	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
590	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
591	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
592	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
593	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
594	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
595	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
596	\end{proof}

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat. This
2576	is the target lemma \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} of the section.
2577	
2578	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2579	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2580	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2581	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2582	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2583	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2584	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2585	
2586	\paragraph{The reduction to two general facts.} The target
2587	\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} is a three-line consequence of two facts about
2588	pullback, exactly as in the Stacks proof of \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}. The
2589	first is that pullback commutes with the tensor product for \emph{all}
2590	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the canonical comparison
2591	\(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N\) is an isomorphism for every \(M, N\)
2592	and every \(f\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}, the general strong-monoidality of
2593	pullback). The second is that pullback preserves the structure sheaf,
2594	\(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, already in
2595	hand). Given an inverse \(N\) with \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), pulling back
2596	yields
2597	\[
2598	  f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N
2599	    \;\cong\; f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2600	    \;\cong\; f^*\mathcal{O}_X
2601	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2602	\]
2603	so \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\). The general comparison
2604	\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} is therefore a genuine isomorphism and the
2605	load-bearing input to \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}.
2606	
2607	\paragraph{Why the general isomorphism is genuine content, not free.} The comparison is
2608	\emph{not} a formal consequence of pullback being \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor.
2609	An (op)lax monoidal functor need not preserve invertible objects: global sections
2610	\(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an
2611	invertible module, so the bare oplax comparison \emph{map} carries no invertibility
2612	information. One must genuinely prove that the comparison \(\delta\) is an
2613	\emph{isomorphism}. This is precisely why \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} is real work
2614	and not a corollary of the oplax comparison map
2615	\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}: the oplax structure supplies \(\delta\) as a
2616	morphism for free (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}), but its invertibility is the entire
2617	content.
2618	
2619	\paragraph{The construction route.} The isomorphism is built on a
2620	\emph{concrete} model of the pullback and transported to the project's abstract
2621	\(f^* = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\) along the uniqueness of left
2622	adjoints --- the same \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) device that closed
2623	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. The construction decomposes into four steps, carried
2624	out in the proof of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} and the sub-lemmas it cites:
2625	\begin{itemize}
2626	  \item[(D1)] \emph{Decomposition.} The presheaf pushforward factors as a fixed-ring
2627	    pushforward followed by restriction of scalars, so its left adjoint --- the
2628	    presheaf pullback --- factors as a topological inverse image \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\)
2629	    followed by extension of scalars (\cref{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}).
2630	  \item[(D2)] \emph{Scalar half is strong (free).} Extension of scalars is strong
2631	    monoidal via the base-change distributivity isomorphism
2632	    \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\); so the comparison
2633	    \(\delta\) for the pullback is an isomorphism \emph{if and only if} the comparison
2634	    \(\delta_0\) for \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is.
2635	  \item[(D3)] \emph{Topological half --- the genuine build.} \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is
2636	    the left Kan extension along \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\);
2637	    pointwise its value at an open \(V\) is a colimit over the comma category
2638	    \((F \downarrow V) = \{U : f^{-1}V \subseteq U\}\). This category is up-directed
2639	    (\(U_1 \cup U_2\) is an upper bound), and the tensor product commutes with filtered
2640	    colimits, so \(\delta_0\) is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback0_tensor_iso}).
2641	  \item[(D4)] \emph{Sheafify and transport.} Sheafify the resulting presheaf-level
2642	    isomorphism --- the unit and tensor factors are reconciled with their sheafified
2643	    forms by the sheafification-monoidality brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) ---
2644	    then transport the strong-monoidal structure from the concrete model to
2645	    \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\) along \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\),
2646	    yielding \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}.
2647	\end{itemize}
2648	The substantial work is concentrated in (D3): the filtered-colimit/tensor interchange for
2649	\(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\)-valued presheaves and the concrete pointwise model of
2650	\(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) are not in Mathlib and require a bottom-up construction. The
2651	remaining inputs --- \(\mathtt{distribBaseChange}\), \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\),
2652	\(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\), and the comparison \emph{map}
2653	\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map} --- are standard.
2654	
2655	\begin{lemma}
2656

2661	  Let \(\varphi\) be a morphism of \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaves,

2663	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\) is \emph{lax monoidal}: it
2664	  carries the comparison
2665	  \[

2680	\begin{proof}
2681	  Restriction of scalars along a homomorphism of ring presheaves is lax monoidal --- the
2682	  comparison sends \((a \otimes b)\) to itself under the identification of the

2685	  presheaf pushforward along \(\varphi\) factors as the composite of these two functors,
2686	  so its lax monoidal structure is the composite of the strong-monoidal structure on the
2687	  first factor with the lax structure on the second; the comparison \(\mu\) is the
2688	  whiskered composite of the two comparisons.

2809	  \]
2810	  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness. Composing a
2811	  strong-monoidal functor with \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\), the comparison \(\delta\) for
2812	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is therefore an isomorphism \emph{if and only if} the
2813	  comparison \(\delta_0\) for \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is. This isolates the topological
2814	  half as the sole genuine obligation.
2815	
2816	  \emph{Step 3 (D3: the topological half --- the genuine build).} The functor
2817	  \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is the left Kan extension
2818	  \(\mathrm{Lan}\,(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\) on underlying
2819	  presheaves (its right adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\) is restriction along
2820	  \(F^{\mathrm{op}}\)). Pointwise, with \(F = (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\),
2821	  \[
2822	    \mathtt{pullback}_0(M \otimes N)(V)
2823	      = \operatorname*{colim}_{(F \downarrow V)} \bigl(M(U) \otimes N(U)\bigr),
2824	    \quad
2825	    (\mathtt{pullback}_0 M \otimes \mathtt{pullback}_0 N)(V)
2826	      = \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} M(U)\Bigr) \otimes \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} N(U)\Bigr).
2827	  \]
2828	  The comma category \((F \downarrow V) = \{U : f^{-1}V \subseteq U\}\) is up-directed
2829	  --- \(U_1 \cup U_2\) is an upper bound of any \(U_1, U_2\) --- and the tensor product
2830	  commutes with filtered colimits (the diagonal \((F\downarrow V) \to (F\downarrow V)
2831	  \times (F \downarrow V)\) is final). Hence the comparison \(\delta_0\) is an
2832	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback0_tensor_iso}). This is the genuine content of the lemma.
2833	
2834	  \emph{Step 4 (D4: sheafify and transport).} Steps 1--3 furnish a strong-monoidal
2835	  structure on the concrete presheaf-level inverse image. Sheafify the resulting presheaf
2836	  isomorphism to the sheaf level --- the unit and tensor factors are reconciled with
2837	  their sheafified forms by the sheafification-monoidality brick
2838	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\), the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\)
2839	  shape used in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. By definition
2840	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)
2841	  is \emph{a} left adjoint of the pushforward; the uniqueness of left adjoints
2842	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) supplies a canonical natural isomorphism from it
2843	  to the concrete model, along which the strong-monoidal structure travels. Its
2844	  tensorator becomes the asserted isomorphism
2845	  \[
2846	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2847	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2848	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
2849	  \]
2850	  natural in \(M\) and \(N\), refining the comparison map
2851	  \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} = \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) of
2852	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}; the abstract pullback is never evaluated sectionwise.
2853	
2854	  No shortcut from the bare oplax map is available: the presheaf pullback is oplax
2855	  monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) and so supplies \(\delta\) as a
2856	  morphism for free, but an (op)lax monoidal functor does not preserve invertible objects
2857	  --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1,
2858	  \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so the isomorphism must be proved, which is
2859	  exactly the work of Steps~1--4.
2860	\end{proof}

2862	\begin{lemma}
2863	[Presheaf pullback factors as topological inverse image then extension of scalars]
2864	  \label{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}
2865	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
2866	  Let \(\varphi\) present a pushforward of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along a
2867	  functor \(F\) of ring presheaves. The presheaf pushforward factors as the fixed-ring
2868	  pushforward followed by restriction of scalars,
2869	  \[
2870	    \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi
2871	      \;=\;
2872	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \circ \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi .
2873	  \]
2874	  Consequently its left adjoint, the presheaf pullback, factors as
2875	  \[
2876	    \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi
2877	      \;\cong\;
2878	    \mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{pullback}_0,
2879	    \qquad
2880	    \mathtt{pullback}_0 := (\mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R).\mathtt{leftAdjoint},
2881	  \]
2882	  where \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is the topological inverse image --- the left Kan
2883	  extension along \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\) --- and
2884	  \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi\) is sectionwise extension of scalars along the
2885	  structure-sheaf ring maps.
2886	\end{lemma}
2887	
2888	\begin{proof}
2889	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
2890	  The pushforward factorisation is the definitional decomposition of the presheaf
2891	  pushforward into a fixed-ring pushforward and a restriction of scalars, the same
2892	  decomposition that supplies the lax structure of
2893	  \cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}. The left adjoint of a composite is the
2894	  composite of the left adjoints in the reverse order; the left adjoint of
2895	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi\), and the
2896	  left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R\) is by definition
2897	  \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\). The displayed isomorphism is the resulting decomposition of the
2898	  pullback, canonical because each factor's adjoint is unique up to canonical
2899	  isomorphism. That \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) is the left Kan extension along
2900	  \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\) follows because its right adjoint
2901	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\) is, on underlying presheaves, restriction along that functor.
2902	\end{proof}
2903	
2904	\begin{lemma}
2905	[The topological inverse image commutes with $\otimes$ (filtered-colimit interchange)]
2906	  \label{lem:pullback0_tensor_iso}
2907	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback0TensorIso}
2908	  \uses{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}
2909	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) be the
2910	  topological inverse image of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules of
2911	  \cref{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition} --- the left Kan extension along
2912	  \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}}\). For arbitrary presheaves of
2913	  modules \(M, N\), the canonical comparison
2914	  \[
2915	    \delta_0 :
2916	    \mathtt{pullback}_0(M \otimes N)
2917	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2918	    \mathtt{pullback}_0 M \otimes \mathtt{pullback}_0 N
2919	  \]
2920	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M, N\).
2921	\end{lemma}
2922	
2923	\begin{proof}
2924	  \uses{lem:pullback_lan_decomposition}
2925	  Evaluate both sides pointwise. As a left Kan extension, \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) has the
2926	  pointwise colimit formula
2927	  \[
2928	    \mathtt{pullback}_0(P)(V)
2929	      \;=\;
2930	    \operatorname*{colim}_{(F \downarrow V)} P(U),
2931	    \qquad F = (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base})^{\mathrm{op}},
2932	  \]
2933	  over the comma category \((F \downarrow V)\), whose objects are the opens \(U\) of
2934	  \(X\) with \(f^{-1}V \subseteq U\). Since the presheaf tensor product is sectionwise,
2935	  \((M \otimes N)(U) = M(U) \otimes N(U)\), one has
2936	  \[
2937	    \mathtt{pullback}_0(M \otimes N)(V)
2938	      = \operatorname*{colim}_{(F \downarrow V)} \bigl(M(U) \otimes N(U)\bigr),
2939	    \quad
2940	    \bigl(\mathtt{pullback}_0 M \otimes \mathtt{pullback}_0 N\bigr)(V)
2941	      = \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} M(U)\Bigr) \otimes \Bigl(\operatorname*{colim} N(U)\Bigr),
2942	  \]
2943	  and \(\delta_0(V)\) is the canonical comparison between them. The comma category
2944	  \((F \downarrow V)\) is up-directed: it is nonempty (the whole space \(X\) contains
2945	  \(f^{-1}V\)) and any two members \(U_1, U_2\) have the upper bound \(U_1 \cup U_2\),
2946	  still containing \(f^{-1}V\). Over a filtered (here up-directed) index the tensor
2947	  product commutes with the colimit --- the diagonal \((F\downarrow V) \to (F\downarrow V)
2948	  \times (F\downarrow V)\) is final, so the colimit of the pointwise tensors agrees with
2949	  the tensor of the colimits --- whence \(\delta_0(V)\) is an isomorphism for every \(V\),
2950	  and \(\delta_0\) is an isomorphism of presheaves, natural in \(M, N\).
2951	
2952	  The pointwise left-Kan-extension model of \(\mathtt{pullback}_0\) and the
2953	  filtered-colimit/tensor interchange for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module-valued presheaves are not
2954	  in Mathlib; this lemma is therefore established bottom-up from the
2955	  left-Kan-extension colimit formula and the filtered-colimit preservation of the
2956	  tensor product.
2957	\end{proof}
2958	
2959	\begin{lemma}
2960	
2961	\leanok
2962	[The sheaf-level pullback--tensor comparison map $\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}$]
2963	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_map}
2964	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorMap}
2965	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2966	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2967	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2968	  There is a canonical comparison \emph{morphism}
2969	  \[
2970	    \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} :
2971	      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,(M \otimes_X N)
2972	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2973	      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M
2974	        \otimes_Y (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N
2975	  \]
2976	  of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). It is a \emph{map only}: in
2977	  general it is not asserted to be an isomorphism.
2978	\end{lemma}
2979	
2980	\begin{proof}
2981	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2982	  The presheaf pullback is oplax monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}),
2983	  so at the presheaf level it carries the comparison morphism
2984	  \[
2985	    \delta : (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B)
2986	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2987	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
2988	        \otimes (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B,
2989	  \]
2990	  where \(\varphi\) is the ring-presheaf map presenting \(f\). One transports \(\delta\)
2991	  through sheafification to the sheaf level, using the same
2992	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) device that supplies Steps 1--2 of
2993	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}: the abstract pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
2994	  agrees, up to canonical natural isomorphism, with the sheafification of the presheaf
2995	  pullback, and likewise the sheafified tensor product \(\otimes_Y\) is the
2996	  sheafification of the presheaf tensor. The right-hand side reconciliation of the unit
2997	  and tensor factors with their sheafified forms is supplied by the
2998	  sheafification-monoidality brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Whiskering and
2999	  composing these canonical isomorphisms with the sheafification of \(\delta\) produces
3000	  the asserted sheaf-level morphism \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\), natural in \(M, N\)
3001	  because each ingredient is. No claim of invertibility is made: \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\)
3002	  is the carrier on which the invertible-case isomorphism argument of
3003	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} acts.
3004	\end{proof}

3006	\begin{lemma}
3007	
3008	\leanok
3009	[Composition coherence of the pushforward unit comparison]
3010	  \label{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
3011	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
3012	  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write
3013	  \(\mathrm{upu}\) for the pushforward (right-adjoint) unit comparison
3014	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\), the canonical map
3015	  \(\mathcal{O} \to f_*(\mathcal{O})\) comparing the structure sheaf of the target with
3016	  the pushforward of the structure sheaf of the source. Then \(\mathrm{upu}\) satisfies
3017	  the composition coherence
3018	  \[
3019	    \mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin{;} f)
3020	      \;=\;
3021	    \mathrm{upu}\,f \;\mathbin{;}\;
3022	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{upu}\,h\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
3023	      (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{hom},
3024	  \]
3025	  where \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)_* \cong f_* \circ h_*\) is the
3026	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pushforward. Sectionwise both sides are nothing
3027	  but the functoriality of the structure-sheaf ring maps, so the identity holds on the
3028	  nose after the comparison of sections. This is the right-adjoint half of the unit
3029	  composition coherence, and it is the input consumed by the mate transport that produces
3030	  the pullback-side coherence \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
3031	\end{lemma}
3032	
3033	\begin{proof}
3034	  Both sides are morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules; it suffices to compare them on
3035	  sections over each open. Each comparison \(\mathrm{upu}\) acts on sections as the
3036	  structure-sheaf ring homomorphism induced by the relevant morphism of schemes, and the
3037	  pseudofunctor coherence \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) acts as the identification of
3038	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)\)-pushforward sections with iterated \(f_*\,h_*\)-pushforward
3039	  sections. The composition coherence is therefore the functoriality identity
3040	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ f^\sharp\) of the structure-sheaf ring maps,
3041	  which holds definitionally; the two sides agree section by section.
3042	\end{proof}

3044	\begin{lemma}
3045	
3046	\leanok
3047	[Composition coherence of the pullback unit comparison]
3048	  \label{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
3049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
3050	  \uses{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
3051	  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write
3052	  \(\mathrm{pbu}\) for the pullback (left-adjoint) unit comparison
3053	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\), the canonical map
3054	  \(f^*\mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}\). Then \(\mathrm{pbu}\) satisfies the composition
3055	  coherence
3056	  \[
3057	    \mathrm{pbu}(h \mathbin{;} f)
3058	      \;=\;
3059	    (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3060	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,h).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
3061	      \mathrm{pbu}\,h,
3062	  \]
3063	  where \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)^* \cong h^* \circ f^*\) is the
3064	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pullback. This is the genuinely-new ingredient
3065	  for \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}: the left-adjoint pullback is defined abstractly, with
3066	  no sectionwise value, so this identity is \emph{not} a sectionwise statement and cannot
3067	  be checked on sections.
3068	\end{lemma}
3069	
3070	\begin{proof}
3071	  \uses{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
3072	  The pullback \(f^*\) is the left adjoint of the pushforward \(f_*\), and likewise for
3073	  \(h\) and \(h \mathbin{;} f\); the comparison \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is the adjunction
3074	  \emph{mate} of the pushforward comparison \(\mathrm{upu}\,f\)
3075	  (\cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}) across the pullback--pushforward adjunction,
3076	  and similarly for \(h\) and \(h \mathbin{;} f\). Since the mate correspondence is a
3077	  bijection, the asserted identity follows once both sides are transposed under the
3078	  adjunction \((h \mathbin{;} f)^* \dashv (h \mathbin{;} f)_*\).
3079	
3080	  Under this transpose the left-hand side \(\mathrm{pbu}(h \mathbin{;} f)\) becomes the
3081	  pushforward comparison \(\mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin{;} f)\). The right-hand side, a
3082	  composite involving \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}\) and the mates of
3083	  \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,h\), transposes --- using the conjugation of the
3084	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\) on the left adjoints
3085	  corresponds to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f\) on the right adjoints) and the unit of
3086	  the composite adjunction \((f^* \dashv f_*) \circ (h^* \dashv h_*)\) --- exactly into the
3087	  right-hand side of the pushforward coherence
3088	  \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}, namely
3089	  \(\mathrm{upu}\,f \mathbin{;} f_*(\mathrm{upu}\,h) \mathbin{;}
3090	  (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{hom}\). Thus the two transposed sides coincide
3091	  by \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}, and the original identity follows by the
3092	  injectivity of the transpose.

3095	\begin{lemma}

3097	\leanok
3098	[Pullback preserves the structure sheaf]
3099	  \label{lem:pullback_unit_iso}

3101	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3102	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes. The pullback of the structure sheaf
3103	  is the structure sheaf: there is a canonical isomorphism
3104	  \[
3105	    f^*\bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
3106	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3107	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,Y.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf},
3108	    \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad

3110	  \]
3111	  where \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf} = \mathcal{O}_X\) is
3112	  the monoidal unit of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
3113	\end{lemma}
3114	
3115	\begin{proof}
3116	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3117	  This is a one-step consequence of a Mathlib instance, with no chart-chase. The
3118	  canonical comparison map is the genuine Mathlib morphism
3119	  \[
3120	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,f :
3121	      f^*\mathcal{O}_X \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
3122	  \]
3123	  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
3124	  proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor
3125	  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}\) on opens is \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
3126	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\).
3127	
3128	  The point is that the finality hypothesis holds \emph{unconditionally}, for every
3129	  morphism of schemes \(f\). The preimage functor \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}
3130	  : \mathtt{Opens}\,X \to \mathtt{Opens}\,Y\) is a frame homomorphism: it preserves
3131	  arbitrary unions and finite intersections, hence preserves all finite limits. A
3132	  functor between small categories that preserves finite limits is representably flat,
3133	  so \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\) gives \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathtt{Final}\)
3134	  with no hypothesis on \(f\) whatsoever. (There is no need to pass to affine charts:
3135	  finality is not a special feature of open immersions but a general property of
3136	  preimage-on-opens.)

3138	  Consequently the instance \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) fires
3139	  directly, and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The asserted
3140	  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the bundled iso
3141	  \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\,f\) of this comparison.

3294	  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
3295	  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). We take the pullback witness
3296	  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\) and exhibit \(f^*M\) as
3297	  \(\otimes\)-invertible by the composite
3298	  \[

3301	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
3302	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;

3311	  identifying \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}).

3313	  the second as the image of an isomorphism under a functor, the third by

3319	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
3320	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
3321	
3322	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
3323	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
3324	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
3325	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
3326	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
3327	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
3328	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
3329	
3330	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
3331	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
3332	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
3333	\[
3334	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
3335	  \;:\equiv\;
3336	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
3337	\]
3338	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
3339	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
3340	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
3341	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
3342	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
3343	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
3344	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
3345	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
3346	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
3347

3323	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
3324	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation

3326	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
3327	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
3328	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
3329	
3330	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
3331	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
3332	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
3333	\[
3334	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
3335	  \;:\equiv\;
3336	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
3337	\]
3338	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
3339	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
3340	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
3341	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
3342	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
3343	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
3344	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
3345	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
3346	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).

3359	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
3360	  \[
3361	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
3362	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3363	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
3364	  \]
3365	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
3366	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
3367	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
3368	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
3369	  coherence is ever required.
3370	\end{lemma}
3371	
3372	\begin{proof}
3373	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
3374	  Immediate specialisation of the \emph{unconditional} associator
3375	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to the invertible arguments \(M, N, P\); no
3376	  hypothesis on the arguments is used. (The earlier route argued via ``invertible

3381	  the general associator is itself unconditional, its single open left-whiskering

3383	  stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.)
3384	\end{proof}
3385	
3386	\begin{definition}
3387	
3388	\leanok
3389	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
3390	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
3391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
3392	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}

3406	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
3407	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of

3409	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects

3419	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms

3423	  \]

3425	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
